original_article:
MELBOURNE (Reuters) — Severe flooding across southeastern Australia has forced the shutdown of major steel manufacturing facilities, with BlueScope Steel Ltd halting production at its Port Kembla Steelworks—the country's largest integrated steel mill.

BlueScope confirmed that floodwaters from the Illawarra region inundated critical infrastructure at the Port Kembla site, including blast furnace cooling systems and raw material storage yards. The company said it could take four to six weeks to fully restore production capacity.

"The scale of flooding has exceeded all historical records for the Illawarra region," said BlueScope CEO Mark Vassella. "Our immediate priority is the safety of our workforce and the structural integrity of our assets. We will provide further guidance on production timelines once flood conditions recede."

The Port Kembla facility produces approximately 2.6 million tonnes of steel annually, supplying construction, automotive, and manufacturing sectors across Australia and the Asia-Pacific region. BlueScope's shares fell 6.3% on the Australian Securities Exchange following the announcement.

The Bureau of Meteorology warned that further rainfall is expected over the coming week, potentially delaying recovery efforts. Insurance Australia Group estimated total industrial losses from the flooding could exceed A$2 billion.
paraphrases:
MELBOURNE — Catastrophic flooding in southeastern Australia has shut down steel production at BlueScope Steel Ltd's Port Kembla Steelworks, the nation's largest integrated steel mill. The company said floodwaters damaged blast furnace cooling systems and raw material yards, with full production recovery expected to take four to six weeks.

BlueScope CEO Mark Vassella said the flooding has exceeded all historical records for the Illawarra region. The Port Kembla facility produces roughly 2.6 million tonnes of steel annually for construction, automotive, and manufacturing customers across Australia and the Asia-Pacific.

BlueScope shares dropped 6.3% on the ASX. The Bureau of Meteorology has forecast additional rainfall over the coming week, which could further delay restoration efforts. Insurance Australia Group estimated total industrial flooding losses could exceed A$2 billion.

MELBOURNE (AP) — Severe flooding has forced BlueScope Steel Ltd to halt production at its Port Kembla Steelworks in New South Wales—Australia's largest steel production facility.

The company said floodwaters from the Illawarra region inundated critical infrastructure including blast furnace cooling systems and storage facilities. CEO Mark Vassella said full restoration could take four to six weeks and described the flooding as unprecedented for the region.

Port Kembla produces approximately 2.6 million tonnes of steel per year, serving the construction, automotive, and industrial sectors in Australia and the broader Asia-Pacific. BlueScope's shares fell 6.3% following the announcement.

The Bureau of Meteorology warned of continued rainfall in the affected region, potentially extending the recovery timeline. Insurance industry estimates put total industrial losses from the flooding event at more than A$2 billion.

Australia's steel manufacturing sector has been dealt a major blow after flooding forced BlueScope Steel Ltd to shut down production at its flagship Port Kembla Steelworks—the country's largest integrated steel operation.

BlueScope confirmed that floodwaters in the Illawarra region damaged blast furnace infrastructure and raw material handling facilities at the New South Wales site. The company expects four to six weeks for a full production restart. Port Kembla produces around 2.6 million tonnes of steel annually, supplying construction, automotive, and manufacturing end markets.

BlueScope CEO Mark Vassella described the flooding severity as beyond all recorded precedent for the region. Shares in the company fell 6.3% on the Australian Securities Exchange. The Bureau of Meteorology has warned of further rainfall, and Insurance Australia Group has estimated total industrial damage in excess of A$2 billion.
